summary: Steve Yegge presents a collection of nine autobiographical vignettes inspired by another blogger he admires, davesecretary. The post is a departure from his usual technical content, instead offering humorous personal anecdotes ranging from embarrassing elevator encounters to giant spiders on his face to accidentally wearing his coworker's pants to Amazon. The stories share themes of physical comedy, awkward social situations, and the absurdity of everyday life. Yegge frames this as 'light reading' while he works on more technically ambitious posts, and the writing showcases his trademark self-deprecating humor and gift for comedic timing.
key_points: Yegge openly admits to being jealous of another writer (davesecretary) and decides to emulate his storytelling style rather than pretend otherwise
The recurring 'DEFECTIVE!' punchline appears twice - once when his brother crushes a chair and once when football fans tear out stadium bleachers
His father's observation that people mishear 'Y' as 'W' when spelling their last name took decades to discover but proves consistently true
The Amazon story reveals early company culture - a small building near a needle exchange with a teriyaki place employees called 'Scaryaki'
Yegge served in the Navy nuclear reactor program in Idaho before his tech career, hinting at a much more varied background than typical programmers
money_quotes: I'm guessing this is more or less what heroin is like. Some days I don't even shower.
He writes way better than I do, and I'm just going to copy him blatantly, like Terry Brooks rewriting Lord of the Rings line-by-line badly as Shitstorm of Shannara.
All he could think was: This chair is DEFECTIVE!
Falling-asleep voice and tiny voice were both, like, 'Oh, shit!'
Dave barges in, wild-eyed and soaking wet and yelling at us at the top of his lungs... 'DIDN'T ANY OF YOU HEAR ME? I WAS STUCK IN THE GARBAGE CAN!'
Jacob suddenly announces, in a really loud voice that everyone within a block or so can hear: 'You're wearing my pants!'
I said very carefully: 'e', and he wrote 'i', completing my last name as 'Wijji'. It was Jiffily Lubriciously Awesome.
key_insight: Good storytelling requires honesty about influences and willingness to be vulnerable about embarrassing moments - the funniest stories come from situations where you looked like an idiot.
